nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found among Diadema urchin spines, staghorn corals, seagrass beds, and whip corals at depths of 2–42 m.

diet.notes: Has been reported to feed primarily on minute crustaceans, zooplankton, copepods, amphipods, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Tends to require live food to thrive; many specimens have been observed refusing frozen or prepared foods, particularly early in captivity. Feeding 3–4 times per day is generally advised, with food enriched in omega-3 and vitamins when possible.

summary: An unusual reef species that swims vertically head-down among urchin spines and staghorn corals. Generally considered expert-only due to its need for live foods and a mature, microlife-rich aquarium; best kept in schools of five or more with similarly calm tankmates.
careNotes: The Striped Shrimpfish (Aeoliscus strigatus) is a fascinating but challenging saltwater species. It swims in a distinctive vertical posture, snout pointing downward, and has been observed schooling naturally among the spines of Diadema sea urchins and staghorn corals for camouflage and protection. In captivity, a mature, well-established aquarium with abundant copepods, amphipods, and other microinvertebrates is generally considered essential. Specimens tend to require live food—brine shrimp, grass shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae—and have been known to refuse frozen or prepared foods, particularly when first introduced. Feeding 3–4 times daily is commonly advised, with vitamin-enriched live foods when possible. Automated live food feeders or a thriving refugium can help sustain adequate prey populations. The species reacts poorly to aggression from other fish; keeping it only with calm, similarly-sized tankmates such as pipefish and seahorses is generally recommended. The coloration has been reported to vary by habitat: greenish-yellow with diffuse stripes in seagrass environments, and pale with black stripes on sandy/rubble substrates. Water parameter cross-source note: LiveAquaria lists temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase wild data indicates 25.2–29.3°C; the recorded values here (23.7–27.5°C) are the computed average. Tank size disagreement: LiveAquaria recommends 50 gal (189 L), BlueZoo 30 gal (114 L), ReefApp 200 L; the 170 L value here is the average of all three.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15.0 cm max), taxonomy (Centriscidae / Syngnathiformes), native range, and temperature range (25.2–29.3°C from wild data). Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria values.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6 in = 15.2 cm) and tank minimum (30 gal = 114 L). Tank size lower than LiveAquaria (50 gal); averaged with LiveAquaria and ReefApp values.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (15 cm), salinity (SG 1.026), temperature (~26°C), minimum tank (200 L), grouping (5+), diet (zooplankton, live crustaceans), and reef-safe status. Expanded native range to include Red Sea.
